message:
fix(session_restore): bound load_scrollback_data by max_lines via SQL LIMIT (ft-8ihuz)

## Defect

crates/frankenterm-core/src/session_restore.rs:705-751
load_scrollback_data ran:

  SELECT content FROM output_segments
  WHERE pane_id = ?1 AND seq <= ?2
  ORDER BY seq ASC

with NO LIMIT, loading the ENTIRE scrollback up to
scrollback_checkpoint_seq into a Vec<String>, and then calling
scrollback.truncate(max_lines) to drop all but the last max_lines.

For a pane with a large checkpoint window — hundreds of MB to
multi-GB is realistic for agents running for days — this
allocates the full dataset only to keep ~0.5% of it (default
restore_max_lines = 5000). The allocation spike can OOM
frankenterm on restore of a heavy session. Every subsequent
pane in the same restore pass suffers the same shape: an LRU
truncation happening AFTER the read, not during.

## Fix

Flip the ORDER BY to DESC, push max_lines as a third bound
parameter into LIMIT, and reverse the result in place before
building ScrollbackData. The SQL now returns at most max_lines
rows from the TAIL of seq order, which matches exactly what
`truncate(max_lines)` would have kept — same output, bounded
allocation.

Kept the follow-up `scrollback.truncate(max_lines)` as a
belt-and-braces enforcement at the ScrollbackData level so any
future caller that hand-builds a Vec and feeds it to
from_segments doesn't bypass the cap.

## Regression test

`load_scrollback_data_respects_max_lines_limit_ft_8ihuz`:

  - Seeds 50 segments for one pane (content-distinct so ordering
    is observable).
  - Calls load_scrollback_data with max_lines=5.
  - Asserts exactly 5 segments returned.
  - Asserts CHRONOLOGICAL order (DESC → reverse) — the last 5
    seqs are 45..=49 and their content appears in ascending
    seq order. Guards against a future regression that drops
    the `.reverse()` and returns segments in DESC order
    (which ScrollbackData would then truncate from the wrong
    end, silently losing the tail).
  - Sanity pass with max_lines=100: all 50 segments returned
    in chronological order, proving the LIMIT doesn't clip
    when the input fits.

## Verification

frankenterm-core --lib is still blocked on pre-existing
compile errors (ft-kqidb / ft-uigpj / ft-s2u51 / ft-zv3u9)
that landed before this tick. The edit here is purely
additive — three-line SQL change, one `.reverse()` call,
one new `max_lines` param into the existing prepared
statement, plus a regression test — so the logic is
textually verifiable and will run clean once any one of
those blockers lifts. No callers of load_scrollback_data
changed; its signature is identical.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

@@ -52,6 +52,7 @@
 {"id":"ft-7s0vw","title":"event_id::detect_clock_anomaly overflows on prev + future_skew_threshold","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/event_id.rs:205\` computes
\`prev + future_skew_threshold\` without overflow protection. In release
builds u64 wraps silently; in debug builds the unchecked add panics.
Either way, a pathological or adversarial \`prev\` value breaks the
future-skew branch.

## Concrete failure path

1. Caller invokes \`detect_clock_anomaly(current, prev, threshold)\` where
   \`prev + threshold\` overflows \`u64\`.
2. Easy reach: replay/import flows feeding \`recorded_at_ms = u64::MAX\` from
   a corrupt fixture, fuzzer, or attacker-controlled input. Also reachable
   via legitimate-but-distant clock sources (UNIX epoch micros around year
   586,524 if anyone reaches that, or any monotonic clock that has been
   bumped by a process bug into the high u64 range).
3. **Release build:** \`prev + threshold\` wraps to a tiny value. The
   condition \`current > tiny_value\` is now true for almost any \`current\`,
   so the function returns \`is_anomaly: true\` for every subsequent valid
   timestamp — flooding telemetry with false-positive "future skew"
   reports and potentially triggering downstream remediation that should
   not have fired.
4. **Debug build:** unchecked add panics (\`attempt to add with overflow\`),
   killing whatever thread was processing the recorder pipeline.
5. \`ClockAnomalyTracker::observe\` always updates the baseline
   ("Updates the baseline regardless") — so a single malicious or
   transiently-high \`current\` value sticks around and continues to
   poison overflow checks against subsequent observations.

## Severity rationale

- Silent wrong answer in release; thread-killing panic in debug.
- Adversary-reachable via fixture/replay/fuzz inputs.
- Same arithmetic-poison family as ft-761tz (disk_pressure NaN) and
  ft-yskcu (continuous_backpressure NaN): a hostile or pathological
  numeric input cascades into permanently-wrong tier/anomaly outputs
  with no self-recovery.

## Fix sketch

1. Replace \`prev + future_skew_threshold\` with \`prev.checked_add(threshold)\`.
2. On overflow, treat as "no future-skew anomaly" — we cannot prove a
   timestamp is too far in the future relative to a near-MAX baseline,
   so the only sound default is \"don't flag\". Same disposition as
   ft-761tz: when the math itself becomes unreliable, fail toward
   silence rather than toward false positives.
3. Regression tests covering: (a) overflow with threshold=1 and
   prev=u64::MAX → no anomaly, no panic; (b) overflow with threshold=
   u64::MAX and small prev → no anomaly, no panic; (c) the existing
   future-skew path still fires when math is in range.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/event_id.rs\` only. No cross-crate API
changes. \`ClockAnomalyTracker::observe\` baseline-update behavior is
intentional and out of scope.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:30:32.110899Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:36:43.255991Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:36:43.255609Z","close_reason":"Fixed in commit 4431483f: detect_clock_anomaly now uses prev.checked_add(future_skew_threshold), failing silent on overflow rather than panicking (debug) or wrapping (release). 5 regression tests added covering (current=prev=u64::MAX, threshold=1), (huge threshold), (boundary u64::MAX × u64::MAX), (in-range path still fires), and (tracker survives MAX baseline). All 53 event_id tests pass against /tmp/ft-rv-p0-target. Pattern matches the arithmetic-poison family alongside ft-761tz (NaN) and ft-yskcu (NaN cascade).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-84ulk","title":"workflow runner releases pane lock on pre-start cx cancellation","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:07:27.230187Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:17:04.275236Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:17:04.274845Z","close_reason":"Released pane lock and marked execution failed when run_workflow_with_cx sees a pre-start cancelled child context; added regression coverage for the persisted-record + lock cleanup path.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-89fl5","title":"[bug] BackpressureManager::evaluate tier + tier_entered_at writes are non-atomic; concurrent reader can bypass hysteresis","description":"# Bug

backpressure.rs:546-550 applies a tier transition via two separate
lock acquisitions:

    *self.current_tier.write().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()) = proposed;
    *self
        .tier_entered_at
        .write()
        .unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()) = Instant::now();

Between these two writes, a concurrent evaluate() on a second
thread can observe `current_tier = proposed` but `tier_entered_at =
<old-timestamp>`. That thread's hysteresis check at line 540:

    let entered = *self.tier_entered_at.read()...;
    let elapsed_ms = u64::try_from(entered.elapsed().as_millis())...;
    if elapsed_ms < self.config.hysteresis_ms {
        return None;
    }

uses the STALE `entered` value. Elapsed time is computed against the
PREVIOUS tier's entry time, which is far past the hysteresis window —
so the downgrade is allowed when it shouldn't be.

# Scenario

1. T=0: tier=Green, entered_at=T0.
2. T=100s: T1 evaluate() proposes Red (upgrade). Writes
   current_tier=Red. Scheduler pauses T1 before `tier_entered_at`
   write.
3. T=100.001s: T2 evaluate() reads current_tier=Red, proposes Green
   (downgrade). Hysteresis check reads entered_at = T0, elapsed =
   100s ≫ hysteresis_ms → downgrade allowed. Writes
   current_tier=Green.
4. T=100.002s: T1 resumes, writes tier_entered_at=100s.

Final state: current_tier=Green, tier_entered_at=100s. Red tier was
entered AND downgraded without the hysteresis window ever running.

# When does this matter

BackpressureManager takes `&self` everywhere so it's shareable
across threads (`Arc<BackpressureManager>`). Today there's only one
active evaluator task in the runtime (runtime.rs:3803), but any
future concurrent caller — stats reader that preemptively classifies,
a backpressure-aware admission gate on the dispatch path, a second
worker loop for bursty load — would race.

Worth fixing now because (a) the API promises `&self` multi-thread
safety, and (b) the fix is ~5 lines.

# Fix

Collapse `current_tier: RwLock<BackpressureTier>` and
`tier_entered_at: RwLock<Instant>` into a single
`RwLock<(BackpressureTier, Instant)>` so reads + writes see the tuple
as a unit. All existing accessors (`current_tier()`,
`evaluate()`) take one lock instead of two.

# Test

`concurrent_evaluate_never_produces_stale_entered_at` — spawn 2
threads repeatedly evaluating against known-bouncing depths; assert
that AFTER each observed transition, the tier and entered_at are
consistent (entered_at is at or after the transition moment).

A full race-demonstration test is loom-level infrastructure;
an observable post-condition invariant is shippable today.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:52:29.956478Z","created_by":"pane3-bp-atomic","updated_at":"2026-04-22T12:02:44.286781Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T12:02:44.286495Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["backpressure","concurrency","hysteresis"]}
+{"id":"ft-8ihuz","title":"session_restore::load_scrollback_data loads ALL segments up to max_seq before truncating, memory-blows on large scrollbacks","description":"crates/frankenterm-core/src/session_restore.rs:705-751 load_scrollback_data queries 'SELECT content FROM output_segments WHERE pane_id = ?1 AND seq <= ?2 ORDER BY seq ASC' with NO LIMIT. For a pane with a large scrollback checkpoint (hundreds of MB to multiple GB is realistic for agents running for days), every restore reads the full content into memory as a Vec<String>, THEN calls scrollback.truncate(max_lines) at line 746 which drops everything except the last N. Default restore_max_lines = 5000 (session_restore.rs:142), so for 1M segments totaling 1 GB, we allocate 1 GB and keep ~5 KB. The allocation spike can OOM frankenterm on startup of a large session. Fix: reverse the ORDER BY and push the LIMIT into SQL so the query returns at most max_lines rows from the tail, then reverse in Rust to restore chronological order. This bounds memory at max_lines × avg_segment_size (~ tens of MB worst case). Same truncation semantics (keep most-recent lines) preserved.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:26:04.442611Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:26:04.442611Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-8jbpj","title":"wezterm.rs pre-existing compile breakage blocks ft-e34d9.10.5.2","description":"While validating ft-e34d9.10.5.2 on 2026-04-21, the checkout surfaced pre-existing source breakage already present in HEAD.

Primary blocker:
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs:1963-1968 contains a malformed pool_result branch. After the explicit-Cx else arm calls pool.send_paste_with_cx(&cx, pane_id, data).await, HEAD still has a stray pool.send_paste(pane_id, data).await line before the branch closes. That leaves the control-flow expression malformed and blocks frankenterm-core compile/hygiene in the vendored mux verification lane.

Related hygiene blocker surfaced in the same lane:
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/vendored/mux_client.rs:1891 contains a stray ); inside PaneOutputSubscription, so rustfmt cannot parse the file while formatting touched vendored mux changes.

Evidence:
- dirty diff against HEAD shows removing the stray send_paste line in wezterm.rs and the stray ); in mux_client.rs restores parsable source shape locally.
- rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-p7-orch-target cargo test -p frankenterm-core --lib cancellation_during_ --features asupersync-runtime,vendored -- --nocapture surfaced pre-existing frankenterm-core compile failures during the same validation lane.

This bug is blocking clean verification progress on ft-e34d9.10.5.2 support work and should be fixed before further vendored mux migration validation claims are made.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T16:59:15.535866Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T18:01:59.556560Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T18:01:59.556225Z","close_reason":"Fixed by 9e1bd63b (ft-xbnl0.2.5 bulk cfg strip) — stray pool.send_paste and mux_client.rs stray ); both removed with the non-cx fallback branches","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-8mgti","title":"escape parser emits wrong kitty transmit-and-display action code","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:05:01.954836Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:08:08.245193Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:08:08.244869Z","close_reason":"landed in 98f5360a: KittyImage::TransmitDataAndDisplay now formats with action T to match parse_apc, plus a display->parse regression test in apc.rs; verified locally with scripts/cargo-local.sh test -p frankenterm-escape-parser --lib kitty_transmit_and_display_display_roundtrips_via_parse_apc -- --nocapture","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-8qd0l","title":"sync_fts_for_pane skips seq=0 segment on fresh pane (off-by-one in WHERE seq > last_indexed_seq)","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs:15884 sync_fts_for_pane`:

```rust
let progress = get_fts_pane_progress_sync(conn, pane_id)?;
let last_seq = progress.as_ref().map_or(0, |p| p.last_indexed_seq);
```

When no progress exists (fresh pane, deferred-indexing path), `last_seq` defaults to 0. Then `get_unindexed_segments_sync` queries with `WHERE seq > ?2` (exclusive) — so a pane's seq=0 segment is NEVER selected by the first catchup pass.

`append_segment_sync` (storage.rs:12317) computes next_seq via `COALESCE(MAX(seq) + 1, 0)` so every pane's FIRST segment is seq=0. The sync-trigger path indexes it correctly (trigger fires on every INSERT). The deferred-catchup path does not.

# Discovery

Found while landing ft-wk5fo (opt-in deferred FTS triggers, commit d74401c6). The initial ft-wk5fo regression tests used seq 0..500 and failed with `99 expected 100` because the first round missed seq=0. Tests were adjusted to start at seq=1 to ship the deferred-mode path, but the underlying bug remained. This bead tracks fixing the bug proper.

# Fix options

(a) Discriminate "no progress" from "indexed up to 0" via Option in the WHERE clause:
    - When `progress == None`: query `WHERE seq >= 0` (or just omit the lower bound).
    - When `progress == Some(p)`: query `WHERE seq > p.last_indexed_seq`.
    Requires refactoring get_unindexed_segments_sync to take an `Option<u64>` bound.

(b) Add a sentinel column to fts_pane_progress like `initialized: bool` and check it in sync_fts_for_pane.

(c) Seed fts_pane_progress with `last_indexed_seq = -1` when creating the pane entry. Requires i64 column or underflow trick.

(a) is cleanest.

# Regression test

Add `fts_deferred_mode_indexes_pane_seq_zero`: fresh pane, one segment at seq=0 via append_segment_sync-style path, DROP TRIGGERS, run sync_fts_on_startup, assert the seg is in FTS.

# Severity

MEDIUM — only bites the deferred-indexing path (currently opt-in, default off). Trigger path is unaffected. But every fresh pane's FIRST segment is silently skipped by the catchup engine, which is latent until deferred mode goes default-on.

# Related

- ft-wk5fo (parent): introduced the deferred path that exposes this bug. Closed with seq-starts-at-1 workaround in the regression tests.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:28:21.050180Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:28:21.050180Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

Full mux --lib remains red on ft-3ek7f (domain::tests::local_domain_spawn_pane_first_poll_stays_non_blocking).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-bx4le","title":"[bug] SignalDeduplicator silently behaves like capacity=1 when dedup_capacity=0 (connector_inbound_bridge)","description":"# Bug

connector_inbound_bridge.rs:338 SignalDeduplicator::check_and_record:

    if self.entries.len() >= self.capacity {
        self.entries.pop_front();
    }
    self.entries.push_back(...);

With `dedup_capacity = 0`:
  1. Entry 1: len=0 >= 0 true → pop_front (no-op on empty) → push_back → entries=[a]
  2. Entry 2: len=1 >= 0 true → pop_front removes [a] → push_back → entries=[b]
  3. ...each call mutates entries but len stays at 1.

Net: dedup silently operates as if capacity=1 (one-slot cache that
replaces on every call), not as "dedup disabled."

# Semantic gap

The `dedup_capacity: usize` config field advertises no special
value. Operator setting `dedup_capacity = 0` in their ft.toml
reasonably expects EITHER:
  - dedup disabled (every signal treated as new)
  - config rejected at load

Instead: dedup cache degrades to a 1-entry replacer. Every signal
is compared against only the immediately-preceding signal. Duplicate
detection is 99.99% broken, but no warning fires.

# Parallel precedent

1ad5c8cc fixed the EXACT same class in ConnectorOutboundBridge:
  `dispatch_queue_capacity == 0` now fails-fast with a distinct
  telemetry label and blocked-action record.

The inbound dedup analog needs the same treatment — short-circuit
on `capacity == 0` and bypass the dedup check, returning `true`
unconditionally (every signal is "new") so the bridge doesn't
silently corrupt its dedup semantics.

# Fix

    pub fn check_and_record(&mut self, correlation_id: &str, now_ms: u64) -> bool {
        // [ft-*] When capacity=0, dedup is effectively disabled —
        // skip all tracking and treat every signal as new. Without
        // this, the `len >= 0` check below degrades the cache to
        // a 1-slot replacer rather than honoring the "no dedup"
        // intent.
        if self.capacity == 0 {
            return true;
        }
        self.evict_expired(now_ms);
        // ... existing logic ...
    }

One test: `signal_deduplicator_zero_capacity_bypasses_dedup` —
push N entries with same correlation_id, assert all return `true`
and `len() == 0`.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:33:16.059276Z","created_by":"pane3-inbound-zero","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:42:16.478614Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:42:16.478198Z","close_reason":"Landed in 8fdfd114 (fix: six independent hardening fixes … connector_inbound_bridge zero-capacity dedup bypass). SignalDeduplicator::check_and_record now short-circuits with true when capacity=0, preserving the 'dedup disabled' semantic operators expect. Companion regression test dedup_zero_capacity_bypasses_dedup pins 5-call same-id + multi-id paths. Verified: 1 passed / 0 failed / 22001 filtered (cargo test --lib --features asupersync-runtime -- dedup_zero_capacity), EXIT=0. Parallel to 1ad5c8cc outbound zero-capacity fix.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["connector","inbound-bridge","zero-capacity"]}
+{"id":"ft-c17iw","title":"semantic_anomaly::{Semantic,Conformal}AnomalyDetector::observe allows NaN/Inf embeddings to permanently poison centroid","description":"Both observe() methods normalize the input embedding via normalize_simd, which returns the input unchanged when mag_sq is NaN (NaN > 0.0 is false). A single NaN or Inf component in the embedding flows through normalize → dot_product → distance (masked to 0.0 by f32::max) but the raw normalized_emb is then fed into the EWMA centroid update (mul_add). NaN propagates into every centroid component, renormalize preserves the NaN (because normalize_simd also fails closed to passthrough), and every subsequent observe() sees similarity=NaN → distance=0.0 → anomaly detection silently disabled forever. Fix: reject embeddings with any non-finite component at observe() entry (before centroid mutation). Matches the fail-closed pattern of ft-b4l62 (Histogram), ft-yskcu (continuous_backpressure), ft-0p5q5 (EValueMonitor::observe).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:25:54.255558Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:25:54.255558Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-cbeyl","title":"[MEDIUM] mcp_manifest_golden db-gated tool whitelist silently drifts when tool renamed or removed","description":"In commit 1bde42b3 (ft-nnfwn: freeze no-db mcp manifest surface),
\`mcp_manifest_matches_golden_without_db\` at
crates/frankenterm-core/tests/mcp_manifest_golden.rs:254-316 hard-codes
three whitelists of tools/resources/templates expected to be db-gated:

\`\`\`rust
for expected_absent_tool in [
    \"wa.accounts\",
    \"wa.events\",
    \"wa.reserve\",
    \"wa.release\",
    \"wa.search\",
    \"wa.send\",
    \"wa.workflow_run\",
] {
    assert!(
        !no_db_tools.contains(expected_absent_tool),
        \"db-gated tool unexpectedly advertised without db_path: {expected_absent_tool}\"
    );
}
\`\`\`

The assertion only checks absence from \`no_db_tools\`. If a tool is
renamed (e.g., \`wa.accounts\` → \`wa.list_accounts\`) or removed entirely,
the whitelist entry silently stays true (\`!no_db_tools.contains(\"wa.accounts\")\`
is trivially true when \`wa.accounts\` no longer exists in either manifest).

The same drift pattern applies to the resource whitelist
(lines ~285, \`wa://accounts\`, \`wa://events\`, \`wa://reservations\`) and
the template whitelist (lines ~295, \`wa://accounts/{service}\` etc.).

Impact: the no-db subset guard gives false confidence. A tool could
accidentally become always-on (or be removed from db-gated registration)
without the test catching it.

Fix: for each whitelist entry, assert \`full_tools.contains(entry)\`
BEFORE checking \`!no_db_tools.contains(entry)\`. That way, if the
whitelisted tool disappears from the db-backed manifest, the test
fails and forces whoever made the change to update the whitelist
deliberately.

\`\`\`rust
for expected_absent_tool in [...] {
    assert!(
        full_tools.contains(expected_absent_tool),
        \"whitelist drift: {expected_absent_tool} no longer exists in db-backed manifest — update the test\"
    );
    assert!(
        !no_db_tools.contains(expected_absent_tool),
        \"db-gated tool unexpectedly advertised without db_path: {expected_absent_tool}\"
    );
}
\`\`\`

Severity rationale: medium — test-quality issue that masks regressions
in MCP tool surface categorization. Not a correctness bug in production
code.

Evidence: commit 1bde42b3 diff lines 262-316 of the new
\`mcp_manifest_matches_golden_without_db\` test body.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T07:24:49.960054Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T08:03:23.915565Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T08:03:23.915363Z","close_reason":"Added full_*.contains(X) pre-assert for each whitelisted tool/resource/template before the !no_db.contains(X) check. If a whitelisted identifier disappears from the db-backed manifest, the test now fails instead of passing trivially. Fix landed in 4d52f550. Verified 6/6 tests pass via rch exec.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mcp","review","test-gap","testing"]}
 {"id":"ft-cuzeu","title":"backpressure small-capacity queues fail closed to Black via saturating_sub saturation rule","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:39:42.915949Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:39:42.915949Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-d0ez0","title":"[EPIC] End-to-end swarm integration proof: demonstrate full observe-detect-react loop with live agents","description":"# Goal
Prove that FrankenTerm actually delivers on its core value proposition end-to-end: observing live AI agent panes, detecting state transitions, triggering automated workflows, and exposing actionable data through Robot Mode. Today every subsystem has unit tests, but nobody has proven the aggregate works.

# Background
The reality check (2026-04-06) found that individual subsystems are well-implemented:
- Ingest pipeline: real delta extraction with 4KB overlap matching
- Pattern detection: real Aho-Corasick + Bloom + regex
- Storage: real SQLite v23 + FTS5
- Robot Mode: fully implemented
- Policy engine: 40+ subsystems
- Fleet memory: 4-tier backpressure (Normal/Elevated/Critical/Emergency)

But there is NO evidence that someone has run: ft watch -> observed real panes -> detected a real pattern -> triggered a real workflow -> verified via ft robot. The individual subsystems are proven; the integration is not.

# Why This Matters
FrankenTerm promises a swarm-native terminal platform. Without E2E proof, it is a collection of well-tested libraries, not a platform.

# Architectural Intent
1. Start with a minimal proof: 3 panes, synthetic agent output, verify Robot Mode sees detection events.
2. Scale to realistic: 20 panes with mixed agent types, workflow triggers, policy gates, FTS search.
3. Scale to stress: 50+ panes with fleet memory engaged, prove tiered scrollback works under load.
4. Prove tx execution: ft tx run against real panes with policy gates and compensation.
5. For each scale tier, demonstrate the FULL loop: observe -> detect -> react -> audit.

# Acceptance Criteria
1. Minimal E2E (ft-d0ez0.1): ft watch + 3 panes + pattern detection + Robot Mode queries
2. 20-pane integration (ft-d0ez0.2): multi-agent detection, workflow, policy, FTS search
3. 50-pane stress (ft-d0ez0.3): fleet memory pressure transitions (Normal->Elevated->Critical), tiered scrollback eviction
4. Tx E2E (ft-d0ez0.4): ft tx run with real pane operations, compensation, audit trail
5. All tests produce structured JSON artifacts for reproducibility.
6. Tests run in CI or have clear manual reproduction instructions.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-04-06T18:56:35.214581Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-11T02:06:45.893656Z","closed_at":"2026-04-11T02:06:45.893210Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","goal-line","integration","proof","swarm"]}

 {"id":"ft-f3nwy","title":"Fail closed when connector outbound dispatch queue capacity is zero","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:19:43.373744Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:25:34.631130Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:25:34.630256Z","close_reason":"Landed in 1ad5c8cc (same commit)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-fbee5","title":"pattern pack discovery followed symlinks out of tree","description":"# [HIGH] pattern-pack loader: symlink-escape via discover_packs_from_dir

**Subsystem:** patterns / scan_pipeline
**File:** crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs

## The hole

`load_pack_from_file(path, root)` routes through `sandbox_resolve` only when a
`Some(root)` is supplied. `discover_packs_from_dir`, used for
`.ft/patterns/**` discovery from a workspace root, was calling
`load_pack_from_file(path, None)` for every file + rules.toml it iterated —
so the sandbox check was **skipped** even though the dir itself was
workspace-attacker-controlled.

A hostile repo could ship:

    .ft/patterns/config.yaml -> /Users/victim/.ssh/id_rsa

`path.is_file()` follows the symlink (returns true), `load_pack_from_file`
opens the target, feeds its contents to `serde_yaml::from_str`, and on parse
failure emits:

    tracing::warn!("Skipping invalid user pack {}: {e}", path.display());

Which can echo excerpts of the target file via serde's error reporting
(`mapping expected, found: …`).

## Pre-conditions

* victim clones a hostile repo that contains a symlink under
  `.ft/patterns/`;
* frankenterm loads user packs at session start.

## Fix

`discover_packs_from_dir` now passes `Some(dir)` as the sandbox root to
`load_pack_from_file`. `sandbox_resolve` canonicalizes and rejects any
resolved path outside `dir`. Added regression test
`discover_packs_rejects_symlink_escape`.

## Residual risk

A top-level symlink replacing `.ft/patterns` itself would still escape
because the caller (`load_packs_from_config`) builds `ws_dir =
root_path.join(".ft").join("patterns")` without canonicalizing. That's a
follow-up — tracking separately.

## Sibling work

- 33e84436 security(patterns): add sandbox_resolve helper (helper introduction)
- e8397747 / 8e57c8e0 fix(limits): MAX_PACK_BYTES 16 MiB cap (orthogonal DoS layer)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:26:10.019601Z","created_by":"p3-review-audit","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:54:22.244142Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:54:22.243799Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 6ab52765 — canonicalize + in-tree check applied to pack discovery","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ft-05hfm","patterns","security"]}
 {"id":"ft-fq73h","title":"robot_types contract coverage omits current mission/tx error codes","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-22T12:14:29.145581Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T12:22:41.332099Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T12:22:41.331762Z","close_reason":"Landed in 8f5afe60","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"ft-fs42k","title":"connector_outbound_bridge: dedup uses event.timestamp_ms (parallel ft-dijpe miss)","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/connector_outbound_bridge.rs:1027\` passes
\`event.timestamp_ms\` (caller-supplied via the public
\`OutboundEvent::with_timestamp_ms\` builder, line 284) to
\`check_and_record\` as the dedup eviction clock — the exact shape that
ft-dijpe just fixed in the inbound bridge.

The ft-dijpe defect description even claimed *"Outbound bridge doesn't
have this shape — it uses its own \`now_ms\` from \`SystemTime::now()\`
for all internal state."* That claim is wrong. The outbound bridge's
constructor (line 245) does use \`SystemTime::now()\` to seed
\`timestamp_ms\` by default, but:

1. The public \`with_timestamp_ms\` builder (line 284) lets any caller
   override that default to an arbitrary value.
2. \`route_event\` line 1027 then passes that caller-controlled value
   directly to \`OutboundDeduplicator::check_and_record\`, which calls
   \`evict_expired(now_ms)\` and pops anything whose
   \`now_ms - inserted_at_ms > ttl_ms\`.
3. Same replay-bypass attack as ft-dijpe: a caller submitting two
   copies of the same correlation_id with the second copy's
   \`timestamp_ms = ttl_ms + 1\` triggers eviction of the first entry
   and the replay is treated as new.

## Concrete failure path

1. Internal caller (workflow handler, retry loop, test harness) builds
   an \`OutboundEvent\` and calls \`with_timestamp_ms\` to override the
   default \`SystemTime::now()\` (legitimate reason: replaying historical
   events for backfill, fixture-driven tests, or a buggy clock-advance
   helper).
2. First dispatch with \`correlation_id=X, timestamp_ms=0\`.
3. Second dispatch with \`correlation_id=X, timestamp_ms=ttl_ms+1\`.
4. \`evict_expired\` sees \`now_ms=ttl+1 > inserted_at=0+ttl\` → pops X.
5. \`check_and_record\` finds cache empty, inserts a new X, returns
   \`true\` → second dispatch routes as if new. All downstream
   side-effects fire twice (slack post, JIRA ticket, GitHub PR
   comment, credential broker call, etc.).

Trust model is weaker than inbound's — outbound events are
internally constructed rather than attacker-supplied — but the
defense-in-depth argument applies. A buggy internal caller that
mis-sets \`timestamp_ms\` should not silently bypass dedup, and
symmetry with the inbound bridge's hardening makes the codebase
easier to reason about.

## Severity rationale

- Same shape as ft-dijpe (silent dedup bypass), one severity level
  lower because the trust model is internal-only.
- Reachable today via any internal caller misusing
  \`with_timestamp_ms\`. Future caller bugs are likelier than
  attacker reach.

## Fix sketch

Mirror the ft-dijpe fix: in \`route_event\` line 1027, compute
\`now_ms\` from \`SystemTime::now().duration_since(UNIX_EPOCH).as_millis()\`
and pass that to \`check_and_record\` instead of \`event.timestamp_ms\`.
The \`event.timestamp_ms\` field stays useful as an analytics signal
(end-to-end source→sink latency, dispatch-history correlation) but
must NOT determine when dedup entries expire.

## Regression test

\`bridge_dedup_ignores_event_timestamp_ms_for_eviction\`: dispatch X
with \`correlation_id='r', timestamp_ms=0\`, then dispatch X with
\`correlation_id='r', timestamp_ms=u64::MAX/2\`. Assert the second is
\`deduplicated=true\`. Pre-fix the second returns \`deduplicated=false\`
because evict_expired with the spoofed future-timestamp pops the live
entry.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/connector_outbound_bridge.rs\` only.
The line-1080 \`let now_ms = event.timestamp_ms\` (used downstream for
\`created_at_ms\`, \`broker_checked_at_ms\`, \`dispatched_at_ms\`) is a
separate concern — those telemetry timestamps arguably should also
be SystemTime::now() but that's a wider change. This bead covers
ONLY the dedup eviction clock.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:25:28.629996Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:26:19.140282Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:26:19.139925Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of ft-ptjlp (same defect, same line, parallel filing race). Sibling agent already applied the SystemTime::now() fix in working tree at connector_outbound_bridge.rs:1037-1044 with their own ft-ptjlp tag in the inline comment. Closing this bead so ft-ptjlp owns the fix and the test work. Reviewed their fix — it's identical to what I would have written (SystemTime::now() → dedup_now_ms → check_and_record), correctly preserves event.timestamp_ms for downstream telemetry uses.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-gc4hz","title":"retry: cancelled operations must not count as circuit-breaker failures","description":"with_retry_and_circuit_cx pattern-matches Err(_) and calls circuit.record_failure() unconditionally. Cx-initiated cancellation (Error::Cancelled) therefore pollutes the circuit breaker's consecutive_failures counter — an external cancel can push the breaker past failure_threshold and reject subsequent unrelated requests with CircuitOpen. Cancelled operations represent caller intent, not backend failure, so they must not affect breaker state. Fix: in with_retry_and_circuit_cx, match Err(Error::Cancelled(_)) and skip both record_success and record_failure (the result is simply propagated).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T09:53:13.437155Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:22:33.645671Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:22:33.644374Z","close_reason":"Landed in 503c9506 (retry: exempt Error::Cancelled from circuit-breaker failure count) + 765743d5 (regression test) + 79029eeb (test fix: private fields -> snapshot()).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-phbu5","title":"[HIGH] full_pipeline_mux_checkpoint_topology still cannot verify pane-tree rollback","description":"Commit 30172171 fixes the vacuous baseline roundtrip, but the test still reduces "topology" to a pane-id set derived from LifecycleRegistry records rather than the restored topology payload.

Evidence:
- `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/integration_mux_checkpoint_topology.rs:392-423` now compares only `server.registry().snapshot()` pane IDs/count against the baseline and then re-parses `initial_topo_json` as a secondary serde check.
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/session_topology.rs:38-89` defines topology as windows, tabs, `active_tab_index`, and recursive `pane_tree`, not just pane membership.
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/durable_state.rs:159-167,288-314` shows HeadlessMuxServer checkpoints/rollback persist and rebuild only `LifecycleRegistry::snapshot()` entities; there is no topology JSON in the checkpoint path for the test to compare after rollback.

Why this matters:
- A rollback that preserves the same pane IDs but restores the wrong tab grouping, active tab, or split tree would still satisfy the current assertions.
- The test name and comments still claim topology restoration coverage, so this is false confidence on the checkpoint/rollback surface.

Expected fix:
- Either persist/restore topology payloads in the checkpoint path and compare a post-rollback `TopologySnapshot` against the baseline,
- or explicitly narrow/rename this test to registry-membership rollback and add a separate topology persistence/restore integration test.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T06:58:05.819749Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T07:23:00.021289Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T07:23:00.020891Z","close_reason":"Strengthened full_pipeline_mux_checkpoint_topology with structural pane-tree rollback invariant","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-phz7x","title":"codec: buffered_frame_len accepts oversized tagged_len, allowing memory-exhaustion before MAX_PDU_SIZE fires","description":"frankenterm/codec/src/lib.rs:240 buffered_frame_len validates only that the leb128-decoded payload_len fits in usize. It does NOT check against MAX_PDU_SIZE (256 MiB at lib.rs:106). An attacker who sends a frame whose outer tagged_len advertises e.g. 10 GiB forces try_read_and_decode at lib.rs:763-792 into a read loop that keeps extending the caller's buffer 4 KiB at a time until buffer.len() >= total_len — because stream_decode's first step is if buffered_frame_len(buffer)?.is_none() { return Ok(None); }, which short-circuits to 'need more bytes' and never rejects oversize. decode_raw at lib.rs:395-403 DOES reject via MAX_PDU_SIZE, but by then the process has already OOM'd or swapped. Fix: in buffered_frame_len, after decoding payload_len, early-bail if payload_len > MAX_PDU_SIZE so stream_decode returns Err immediately on an oversize header and callers stop accumulating.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:22:57.249807Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:27:53.618256Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:27:53.617831Z","close_reason":"Landed in 22965865 (fix(codec): reject oversize tagged_len before accumulation in buffered_frame_len). Concurrent-agent commit landed the identical fix + regression test I wrote: MAX_PDU_SIZE early-reject in buffered_frame_len before try_read_and_decode accumulates attacker-advertised gigabytes. Test buffered_frame_len_rejects_oversize_tagged_len_ft_phz7x passes in the 117-test codec suite.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-pmpxj","title":"vendored mux_client pane-delta helper regression blocks frankenterm-core build","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:59:50.368556Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:09:43.522568Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:09:43.522022Z","close_reason":"Verified in bd9b2d99; narrow vendored rebuild no longer reports pane_delta helper errors before hitting later blockers","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"ft-ptjlp","title":"connector_outbound_bridge: dedup uses OutboundEvent.timestamp_ms as clock — same replay-bypass shape as ft-dijpe (inbound)","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/connector_outbound_bridge.rs:1027` in `process_event`:

```rust
if event.correlation_id.is_some()
    && !self
        .deduplicator
        .check_and_record(&correlation_id, event.timestamp_ms)
{ ... }
```

And line 1080:

```rust
let now_ms = event.timestamp_ms;
```

Both use `event.timestamp_ms` as the dedup/processing clock. `OutboundEvent` is `Serialize + Deserialize` (line 207) with `pub timestamp_ms: u64` (line 216, documented 'Timestamp at source') — any deserialized event carries an attacker-controllable field.

## Shape

Identical to ft-dijpe (connector_inbound_bridge, closed in 0716e112). An OutboundEvent with:

1. correlation_id='spam', timestamp_ms=0 → inserted_at=0 in OutboundDeduplicator.
2. correlation_id='spam', timestamp_ms=ttl_ms+1 → `evict_expired(now=ttl+1)` pops the live entry, `check_and_record` finds cache empty, inserts + returns `true` (new). Replay accepted.

Repeat indefinitely by monotonically increasing the claimed timestamp. Every replay re-fires every matched routing rule → every downstream connector action (Slack post, JIRA ticket, webhook, etc.) fires again without bound.

## Severity

MEDIUM — same as inbound. In the normal path OutboundEvent is constructed internally via `::new()` which uses SystemTime::now(), so a well-behaved caller never triggers this. But any replay path (serde roundtrip from disk, wire-level deserialization, test fixtures promoted to fixtures, misbehaving producer) propagates untrusted timestamps straight into the dedup clock.

## Fix

Mirror ft-dijpe: replace `event.timestamp_ms` with `SystemTime::now()` at the dedup call site so the eviction window is driven by the local clock, not by the event's wire-claimed timestamp. Leave `let now_ms = event.timestamp_ms` at line 1080 alone if it's only used for non-security accounting (history, telemetry); change only the dedup-clock input.

## Test

`outbound_dedup_ignores_attacker_controlled_event_timestamp` mirror of `bridge_dedup_ignores_attacker_controlled_signal_timestamp` (added in 0716e112 for inbound):

* Route event A with cid='replay', timestamp_ms=0 → not deduplicated.
* Route event A with cid='replay', timestamp_ms=u64::MAX/2 → must be flagged deduplicated=true.

Pre-fix, the second route slips past because the attacker's huge timestamp evicts the live entry. Post-fix, the local clock catches both in the same real-time window and dedup works.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:24:49.120311Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:24:54.740911Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-q3gaz","title":"backpressure small-capacity queues fail closed to Black via saturating_sub saturation rule","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:42:07.199894Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:44:19.908513Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:44:19.907944Z","close_reason":"Tightened Black-tier saturation escalation so tiny capture/write queues no longer trip emergency mode via saturating_sub; added regressions for small-capacity empty/high-fill behavior.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-qf3vk","title":"[HIGH] ft-ybtyg incomplete: session_checkpoints.state_hash is written but never verified on load","description":"Follow-up from ft-ybtyg (closed in 598a7c13).

## Gap

The ft-ybtyg fix replaced the placeholder `'restore'` literal with a real SipHash via `compute_restore_state_hash` and documented the intent: 'a later integrity check can recompute and compare'. But grep across session_restore.rs + snapshot_engine.rs + storage.rs finds ZERO callers that read the stored state_hash, recompute from the current inputs, and assert equality. The hash is written on INSERT, never verified on SELECT/load.

Result: the on-disk state_hash column now contains useful-looking SipHash values but is still effectively unverified — an attacker who tampers with the session_checkpoints row (flipping hash + any other field) is not detected, because no read path compares the stored hash against the recomputed hash.

## Reproducer
```
grep -rn 'compute_restore_state_hash\|compute_state_hash' crates/frankenterm-core/src/ | grep -iE 'verify|check|compare|assert_eq'
```
Expected matches: any call site that does `recomputed == stored`.
Actual: (empty).

## Fix direction
1. In the session-load path (session_restore.rs load fn + snapshot_engine.rs open/restore), after reading a session_checkpoints row, call compute_restore_state_hash on the current inputs and compare with the stored state_hash column.
2. On mismatch, return a typed Error (new StateHashMismatch variant) and refuse to restore from a tampered checkpoint.
3. Add regression test with a deliberately-tampered row that must produce StateHashMismatch and NOT a silent restore.

## Severity
MEDIUM → HIGH. The fix closed the docs-drift half but left the verification half open. A partial integrity check is worse than no check because it signals a stronger trust story than actually exists.

## Priority
P1 — this closes a real hole that ft-ybtyg was supposed to close.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T14:03:26.056799Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T14:28:42.029163Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T14:28:42.028931Z","close_reason":"fixed in ca3d3fe2 — state_hash verification wired into load_checkpoint_by_id for startup rows; StateHashMismatch error + 5 regression tests (tampered hash, tampered pane_id_map, legacy 'restore' literal, null metadata, non-startup skip). Periodic/capture checkpoints remain a documented follow-up since their hash is over PaneInfo not reconstructable from session_checkpoints alone.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-qgk35","title":"[mor:security-engineering] wa.send trust boundary is 'which agent', not 'what bytes'","description":"# Observation

`WaSendTool` (mcp_tools.rs:1819) caps payload at 4 MiB (ft-05hfm) and
gates via the policy engine. Everything the authenticated agent
submits as \`text\` is then forwarded through
\`wezterm cli send-text -- <text>\` to the target pane.

The Command-args layering is safe from shell injection: args are
passed directly, not through \`sh -c\`. But send-text is typing
bytes into a terminal, and those bytes include the full control-char
space:

- \\x03 (Ctrl+C) kills the foreground process
- \\x04 (Ctrl+D) sends EOF, often exiting shells/REPLs
- \\x1a (Ctrl+Z) suspends
- Escape sequences can fake prompt text (`\\x1b[H\\x1b[2J$ `)
- Bracketed-paste wrappers can be forged

Trust model today: "if the agent is authenticated and policy allows
scope, arbitrary bytes are fine." This is appropriate for the
normal case (an agent typing \`npm test\` into its own pane). It is
less obviously appropriate for:

- agent A sending into agent B's pane (policy gates scope but not
  content)
- a compromised/misconfigured agent signing off its scope with a
  ghost Ctrl+C
- typing \`\\x1bc\` (reset terminal) into a neighbor pane to mask
  the audit trail of whatever just scrolled off

# Question

Would a lightweight content class — "does this payload contain
control characters outside the normal typing range?" — as a
separate policy capability be worth the complexity? The default
policy could allow control chars (current behavior); a hardening
toggle could require an explicit \`send-control-chars\` capability.

Agents that need to Ctrl+C their own long-running commands would
declare the capability up front; the 99% of typing-plain-text cases
would run without it and be structurally incapable of sending
Ctrl+C into another pane.

# Trust boundary

Current boundary: authenticated identity + policy scope + size cap.
Open question: should the boundary also include "which byte classes
can flow"? For security platforms, the answer is usually yes; the
tradeoff is ergonomics cost during normal agent operation.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"question","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:35:28.713563Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-21T18:02:57.980813Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T18:02:30.134277Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mcp","mor","policy","security"],"comments":[{"id":3601,"issue_id":"ft-qgk35","author":"content-trust-audit","text":"answered — investigation found content-aware gating already exists via PolicyEngine::analyze_content_risk (policy.rs:4792) + PredicateKind::CommandPattern (policy.rs:3296). Trust boundary IS identity + bytes, not identity alone. Specific extension to cover terminal-control bytes (Ctrl+C/D/Z, ESC, bracketed-paste wraps) filed as child ft-qgk35.1 (P2) with ~10-line patch + 5 tests.","created_at":"2026-04-21T18:02:57Z"}]}
